excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco's Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it "Bop Spotter," after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots. "This is culture surveillance," he wrote on the Web page.
citations.title: The Artist Exposing the Data We Leave Online

Journalistic accounts of the data-enabled transparency versus risk (e.g., NYT, SF Standard, mission local) illustrate how real-time, public-facing tooling can generate significant civil-liberties considerations, which should be reflected in a safety model that prioritizes harm elimination, data minimization, and transparency. excerpts: **San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission District.**
A solar panel on top of the box keeps the phone powered up.

Bop Spotter isn't much to look at: it's a plastic box attached near the top of a light pole located in the heart of the Mission District — one of San Francisco's most densely populated and diverse neighborhoods.

The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it "Bop Spotter," after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots. "This is culture surveillance," he wrote on the Web page.
citations.title: The Artist Exposing the Data We Leave Online

excerpts: SFMTA is transparent about our transportation data, and will continue to be. But when our staff’s safety, and personal information of people who have received parking citations, is at risk, we must act on that swiftly.

Sept. 23, 2025
**SFMTA statement:**
We made sure that all access to citation data was via authorized routes. SFMTA is transparent
about our transportation data, and will continue to be. But when our staff’s safety, and personal
information of people who have received parking citations, is at risk, we must act on that swiftly.

The official way to access our parking citation data is via our public website on DataSF. Anyone is still able to see type of citation, date of issuance and data that can be mapped and analyzed on DataSF daily.

excerpts: **San Francisco software engineer Riley Walz's Bop Spotter runs the song identifier app Shazam to quietly listen in on what music passersby are listening to in the city's diverse Mission District.**

Bop Spotter isn't much to look at: it's a plastic box attached near the top of a light pole located in the heart of the Mission District — one of San Francisco's most densely populated and diverse neighborhoods.

Finally, linkage to Epstein-era data handling adds a boundary-context for the ethics and privacy of public data repositories, reinforcing the need for robust privacy-preserving design in any Riley Walz-inspired system. Taken together, these excerpts support the view that social scoring or reputational profiling is plausible as a downstream risk, even if not intentionally built as a scoring system by Walz himself, and they underscore the importance of privacy-by-design controls and governance to mitigate such misuse.

The Find My Parking Cops project pages themselves, including statements that describe the site’s function and its limits, provide concrete examples of how public data can be scraped and mapped in real time, contributing to the transparency-safety tension. Finally, articles detailing how the data access and mapping were restricted by the city illustrate practical consequences of balancing openness with privacy and safety, reinforcing the central tensions described in the field value.

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco's Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. excerpts:
See Which Restaurants Have The Most Attractive Diners – _According to AI!_
Search
I scraped millions of Google Maps restaurant reviews, and gave each reviewer's profile picture to an AI model that rates how hot they are out of 10. This map shows how attractive each restaurant's clientele is. Red means hot, blue means not.
The model is certainly biased. It's certainly flawed. But we judge places by the people who go there. We always have. And _are we not also flawed?_ This website just puts reductive numbers on the superficial calculations we make every day. A mirror held up to our collective vanity.
Methodology • Made by [Riley Walz](https://walzr.com)
HOT AGE GENDER
HOT NOT
Available in New York Los Angeles San Francisco
Map data © [OpenStreetMap](https://www.openstreetmap.org/

excerpts:
Sept. 23, 2025
**SFMTA statement:**
We made sure that all access to citation data was via authorized routes. SFMTA is transparent
about our transportation data, and will continue to be. But when our staff’s safety, and personal
information of people who have received parking citations, is at risk, we must act on that swiftly.

We made sure that all access to citation data was via authorized routes. SFMTA is transparent
about our transportation data, and will continue to be. But when our staff’s safety, and personal
information of people who have received parking citations, is at risk, we must act on that swiftly.
The official way to access our parking citation data is via our public website on DataSF. Anyone
is still able to see type of citation, date of issuance and data that can be mapped and analyzed
on DataSF daily.

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco's Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it "Bop Spotter," after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots. "This is culture surveillance," he wrote on the Web page.

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco's Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. basis.reasoning: Data minimization centers on collecting only what is necessary, limiting exposure and access to data, and ensuring data is retrievable only through approved channels. Excerpts that emphasize controlled or authorized access, and that reiterate transparency about how data is accessed, align with the principle by showing intentional boundaries around data use. Specifically, statements that the official path to access parking citation data is via a public website on DataSF, and that access should be via authorized routes, directly support limiting who can see data and through which means, which is core to minimizing data exposure. Similarly, reaffirmations of ongoing transparency about transportation data, coupled with caveats about protecting staff safety and personal information, reinforce the idea that data should be narrowed to essential use and protected accordingly. In contrast, passages that describe a full copy of citation data being accessible on a public or broad front (i.e., not restricted to minimized channels) challenge the data minimization ideal by implying broader exposure than necessary. Descriptions of near real-time data powering apps that scrape or reveal locations of tickets, or of culture-surveillance practices that continuously listen in and upload audio, illustrate data collection beyond minimal necessity and highlight real-world tensions with minimization. Contextual references to consent and public data handling further illuminate the risk landscape and the need for tighter minimization controls. Taken together, the strongest supports for Data Minimization come from explicit statements about authorized access routes and public-facing, purpose-bound data sharing, while weaker or conflicting signals arise from broad data exposure and invasive, real-time data collection practices.

excerpts: SFMTA is transparent about our transportation data, and will continue to be. But when our staff’s safety, and personal information of people who have received parking citations, is at risk, we must act on that swiftly.

Sept. 23, 2025
**SFMTA statement:**
We made sure that all access to citation data was via authorized routes. SFMTA is transparent
about our transportation data, and will continue to be. But when our staff’s safety, and personal
information of people who have received parking citations, is at risk, we must act on that swiftly.

The official way to access our parking citation data is via our public website on DataSF. Anyone is still able to see type of citation, date of issuance and data that can be mapped and analyzed on DataSF daily.

We made sure that all access to citation data was via authorized routes. SFMTA is transparent

information of people who have received parking citations, is at risk, we must act on that swiftly.
The official way to access our parking citation data is via our public website on DataSF. Anyone
is still able to see type of citation, date of issuance and data that can be mapped and analyzed
on DataSF daily.

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco's Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. excerpts: SFMTA is transparent about our transportation data, and will continue to be. But when our staff’s safety, and personal information of people who have received parking citations, is at risk, we must act on that swiftly.

Sept. 23, 2025
**SFMTA statement:**
We made sure that all access to citation data was via authorized routes. SFMTA is transparent
about our transportation data, and will continue to be. But when our staff’s safety, and personal
information of people who have received parking citations, is at risk, we must act on that swiftly.

The official way to access our parking citation data is via our public website on DataSF. Anyone is still able to see type of citation, date of issuance and data that can be mapped and analyzed on DataSF daily.

The official way to access our parking citation data is via our public website on DataSF. Anyone
is still able to see type of citation, date of issuance and data that can be mapped and analyzed
on DataSF daily.

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco's Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. basis.reasoning: Excerpts that explicitly call the project a form of culture surveillance and highlight lack of consent provide direct support for the notion of critical press and negative reactions toward privacy-invasive projects. The statement where Walz describes the project as surveillance and notes no notice or consent underscores ethical concerns and public critique about consent and privacy, which aligns with the notion of negative outcomes and critical reception. Additional excerpts describe ongoing criticism of privacy-invading aspects, such as surreptitious recording of street activity, which strengthens the link to negative press and public discourse about civil liberties, consent, and data use. Related pieces on the Find My Parking Cops project and subsequent official statements further illustrate how data-driven risk, takedown or restrictions, and public safety concerns shape the reaction landscape, which is a facet of “outcomes and reactions” in real-world deployments. While none of the excerpts reproduce the exact quoted sensational line about ranking restaurant patrons by hotness using AI, the gathered material consistently demonstrates critical reception, privacy concerns, and culture-surveillance framing, which collectively support the idea that the project faced notable negative press and debate about ethics and consent. The strongest support comes from explicit characterizations of the project as culture surveillance and lack of consent, paired with references to real-time data handling and public-facing data access limitations that reflect negative reactions in the press and by institutions.

excerpts: Walz installed an Android phone on a pole in San Francisco's Mission neighborhood, where it can take in the sounds of passing pedestrians and cars. The phone runs Shazam continuously, attempting to identify any songs that are detectable, and uploads the noisy clips to a Web site. Walz named it "Bop Spotter," after ShotSpotter, the urban microphones that are (fitfully) used to monitor gunshots. "This is culture surveillance," he wrote on the Web page.
citations.title: The Artist Exposing the Data We Leave Online

content.executive_summary: Riley Walz's public-facing projects are characterized by the creative and provocative use of publicly available data and public-space sensors to create web experiments, art, and pranks. His approach prioritizes rapid prototyping and public commentary over formal safety and ethics protocols, leading to several key patterns of risk. The most significant risks observed are the near-real-time publication of individuals' locations or activities (e.g., 'Find My Parking Cops'), the incidental capture and potential exposure of sensitive Personally Identifiable Information (PII) like ambient speech or license plates (e.g., 'Bop Spotter'), and a general absence of proactive remediation channels like opt-outs or formal takedown processes. While his projects often include non-affiliation disclaimers and some transparency about methods, they frequently push ethical boundaries regarding consent, privacy in public spaces, and the repurposing of public records. In response to these patterns, a detailed safety model is proposed, moving beyond generic guardrails. Its core components include: 1) **Harm-first principles** that prioritize eliminating direct harms like doxxing and real-time tracking. 2) **Technical controls** with specific default settings, such as mandatory publication delays (≥24-72 hours), spatial aggregation (blurring precise locations), aggressive PII redaction, and ephemeral data buffers. 3) **Governance processes**, including mandatory Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs), legal reviews for high-risk projects, the creation of public 'data charters' for transparency, and a formal, SLA-driven workflow for fast remediation and takedown requests.
content.overall_assessment_of_walz_approach:
Riley Walz's projects demonstrate a consistent pattern of pushing ethical and safety boundaries, often justifying the approach as artistic commentary, a prank, or a transparency experiment. Here is a direct assessment of how his work handles or fails to handle specific areas of concern:

*   **Privacy:** Projects frequently compromise individual privacy by aggregating public data into new, higher-risk formats. 'Find My Parking Cops' created near-real-time location feeds of public employees, while 'Bop Spotter' captures ambient audio, including potential conversations, in a public space. The 'J*' suite makes sensitive personal information from public records more accessible. Privacy is generally a secondary consideration to the project's concept.

*   **Consent:** Consent is almost universally absent and often acknowledged as such. The 'Bop Spotter' page explicitly states, "No one notices, no one consents." For 'Find My Parking Cops,' no consent was sought from the tracked officers. The projects operate on the premise that data in the public sphere or public record is fair game, bypassing the ethical consideration of consent from the data subjects.

*   **Public Data:** Walz's work is fundamentally based on the creative reuse of public data, whether from government portals (SFMTA), public APIs (Shazam, YouTube), or released documents (Epstein files). He excels at finding and exploiting publicly accessible but not necessarily 'open' data, demonstrating how information intended for one purpose can be repurposed for another, often revealing systemic vulnerabilities.

*   **Real-time Tracking:** This is the highest-risk pattern observed. 'Find My Parking Cops' provided a near-real-time map of officer locations, which was the primary reason for the swift agency intervention. 'Bop Spotter' logs song data in near-real-time. This focus on immediacy creates significant potential for misuse, such as evasion of law enforcement or harassment.

*   **Social Scoring:** While not a primary feature, some projects border on or enable social scoring. 'LooksMapping' was an explicit attempt to rank individuals by attractiveness using AI. Other projects, by exposing patterns of movement or communication archives, could enable third-party profiling and reputational scoring, even if not implemented by Walz himself.

*   **Non-affiliation & Caveats:** Walz consistently uses non-affiliation disclaimers (e.g., "Not affiliated with the San Francisco government"). He is also often transparent about his methods, sometimes publishing technical details that could facilitate replication. These caveats serve more as legal or brand separation than as effective risk mitigation for the public.

*   **Opt-outs:** Formal opt-out mechanisms are largely absent. There is no clear path for an individual to request their data be removed from projects like 'Bop Spotter' or the archived 'Find My Parking Cops' snapshot. The 'J*' suite is a notable exception, providing contact information for redaction requests, which represents a more mature approach to handling sensitive data.

*   **Source Fragility:** The projects are highly susceptible to source fragility. 'Find My Parking Cops' is the canonical example, lasting only four hours before the SFMTA altered its website to cut off the data feed. This demonstrates that his work often exists at the mercy of the data owners, who can and do exert control.

*   **Takedowns:** Takedowns are typically externally forced rather than voluntarily initiated in response to criticism. The SFMTA's action against 'Find My Parking Cops' was a technical takedown by the data owner. There is no record of Walz proactively taking down projects due to ethical concerns, though he does archive them once they become non-functional.

*   **Public Criticism:** Reception is polarized. Tech-savvy audiences and some media outlets often praise the cleverness, artistic merit, or transparency aspects. However, stakeholders directly impacted (like the SFMTA), privacy advocates, and local news coverage often focus on the significant safety and privacy risks, leading to critical reporting and, in the case of the parking app, direct intervention.

project_inventory.description: A web application that mapped the near-real-time locations and routes of San Francisco parking enforcement officers. It achieved this by scraping predictable citation IDs from the SFMTA’s public citation payment portal and plotting the locations of newly issued tickets.
project_inventory.current_status: Defunct / Archived. The project's live data feed was cut by the SFMTA approximately four hours after its launch on September 23, 2025. The website now displays a static snapshot of the data from that day.
project_inventory.data_sources_and_methods: The project scraped the SFMTA's public citation/payment web pages. It did not use an official API. The method involved predicting and enumerating sequential citation IDs based on an observed pattern, then polling the corresponding URLs to retrieve ticket details as they were published.
project_inventory.data_sensitivity_and_pii_exposure: High. The project exposed the near-real-time locations of government employees (parking officers). The underlying citation data also contained PII-like information such as vehicle make, license plate, and the precise location of the citation, which could identify private citizens and their vehicles.
project_inventory.real_time_characteristics: Yes, the project operated in near-real-time. According to Walz, tickets appeared on the site seconds after they were written, enabling real-time tracking until the data source was disabled.
project_inventory.disclaimers_and_transparency: The project page included the disclaimer: "By Riley Walz. Not affiliated with the San Francisco government or Apple's Find My." After being shut down, a note was added: "This site only worked for 4 hours until the SFMTA cut the data source I was using."
project_inventory.outcomes_and_reactions: The project went viral, receiving coverage from Wired, Mission Local, SFist, and The New York Times. The SFMTA responded swiftly by altering its website to disable the scraping method, citing concerns for staff safety and the personal information of citation recipients. The agency's action effectively shut down the project's primary function.

project_inventory.description: A street installation in San Francisco's Mission District that continuously records ambient audio, uses the Shazam API to identify songs being played, and logs the identified tracks to a public webpage. It is framed as an act of 'culture surveillance'.
project_inventory.current_status: Active. As of the analysis date, the project is featured on walzr.com and appears to be operational, with ongoing media coverage noted from late 2024.
project_inventory.data_sources_and_methods: The system uses a microphone connected to an Android phone inside a plastic box to capture 10-minute audio segments. These are uploaded to a server and processed through the Shazam API for song identification. The data source is direct ambient audio capture from a public space.
project_inventory.data_sensitivity_and_pii_exposure: Medium. While the primary output is song metadata, the system captures raw ambient audio, which could include identifiable human speech or other sensitive background sounds. No consent is obtained from passersby. The creator intentionally keeps the device's exact location a secret.
project_inventory.real_time_characteristics: Near-real-time. The system operates continuously and logs approximately 150 tracks per day, indicating a constant stream of data with a short delay between capture and publication.
project_inventory.disclaimers_and_transparency: The project page describes the project as “culture surveillance” and explicitly states, “No one notices, no one consents.” The creator has been transparent about the technical architecture in interviews with outlets like Wired.
project_inventory.outcomes_and_reactions: The project received widespread media coverage from outlets like NPR, KQED, and Wired, sparking public discussions about surveillance, art, and consent in public spaces. There is no record of any formal agency or platform intervention.

project_inventory.description: A collection of web interfaces that present publicly released documents and data related to the 'Epstein files' using UIs that mimic popular consumer services (e.g., Jmail looks like Gmail, JFlights like a flight tracker). The goal is to make the large corpus of public records more accessible and searchable.
project_inventory.current_status: Active. The project launched in November 2025 and remains accessible at jmail.world and its associated sub-projects.
project_inventory.data_sources_and_methods: The data source is publicly released government documents and records, specifically the 'Epstein Files'. Methods include Optical Character Recognition (OCR) of scanned documents (reportedly using Google's Gemini), data indexing, and packaging the information into custom web interfaces.
project_inventory.data_sensitivity_and_pii_exposure: High. The source documents are public records but contain highly sensitive PII, including names, relationships, and other personal details of individuals, some of whom may be victims. The project repackages this material, increasing its accessibility.
project_inventory.real_time_characteristics: No, this is an archival project for browsing a fixed corpus of historical documents. It is not a real-time system.
project_inventory.disclaimers_and_transparency: The project presents the data as "real emails released by Congress." An 'about' page on jmail.world details a redaction policy for victim identities and explicit imagery and provides contact channels (X, Instagram, TikTok) for redaction requests.
project_inventory.outcomes_and_reactions: The project garnered significant media attention (Wired, Fast Company) and massive public interest, with millions of visits reported. The ease of access it provides has led to ethical controversies and debates in press coverage.

project_inventory.description: A conceptual art project and social experiment that began as a fake restaurant listing on Google Maps. The prank generated significant online buzz and a large waitlist, culminating in a real one-night-only pop-up event.
project_inventory.current_status: Historical. The prank and pop-up event occurred in 2023. It is now documented as a past project.
project_inventory.data_sources_and_methods: The project primarily used social engineering, manipulation of a Google Maps business listing, and social media engagement to create the illusion of a real, exclusive restaurant.
project_inventory.data_sensitivity_and_pii_exposure: Low. The project was a public-facing prank and social experiment. Data involved was primarily public engagement and a waitlist for a fictional event.
project_inventory.real_time_characteristics: Not applicable. It was a social experiment, not a real-time data project.
project_inventory.disclaimers_and_transparency: The project's nature as a prank was its central feature, which was eventually revealed. It was not presented with typical data project disclaimers.
project_inventory.outcomes_and_reactions: The project received coverage from The New York Times and was successful in generating significant public interest, with thousands signing up for the waitlist for the pop-up event.

project_inventory.description: A website that reportedly used Artificial Intelligence to analyze images of restaurant patrons and rank them by attractiveness. It was framed critically in media coverage.
project_inventory.current_status: Uncertain. The project was covered by press in September 2025, but its current operational status is not detailed in the provided context.
project_inventory.data_sources_and_methods: The project likely involved scraping public images from social media or other online sources and processing them through an AI model for attractiveness scoring.
project_inventory.data_sensitivity_and_pii_exposure: High. The project used images of people, likely without their consent, and applied a subjective, potentially biased AI-generated score to them, which constitutes a form of social scoring.
project_inventory.disclaimers_and_transparency: Not specified in the provided context.
project_inventory.outcomes_and_reactions: The project received critical press coverage, with one outlet describing it as an "asinine website ranks SF restaurant patrons by hotness using AI."

project_inventory.description: A web-based archival and curation project dedicated to surfacing and celebrating forgotten early-iPhone era YouTube uploads and other nostalgic internet artifacts.
project_inventory.current_status: Active / Archived. The project is listed as part of Walz's portfolio on his personal website.
project_inventory.data_sources_and_methods: The project utilizes the public YouTube API to find and display publicly available video content, curated through a custom user interface.
project_inventory.data_sensitivity_and_pii_exposure: Low. The project deals with content that was voluntarily uploaded to a public platform (YouTube). PII exposure is limited to what users publicly shared in their videos and profiles.
project_inventory.real_time_characteristics: No, it is an archival project.
project_inventory.disclaimers_and_transparency: No special disclaimers are noted beyond those inherent to browsing public web content on platforms like YouTube.
project_inventory.outcomes_and_reactions: The project has been recognized as part of Walz's body of internet art and covered in outlets such as Wired and The New Yorker.

content.analysis_of_privacy_and_consent:
Riley Walz's projects navigate a complex and often contentious landscape of privacy and consent, frequently leveraging publicly accessible data without the explicit consent of the individuals involved. The approach varies by project, but a common thread is the prioritization of rapid, provocative experimentation over formal privacy-by-design principles.

**1. Bop Spotter (Ambient Audio Song Detection):**
*   **Data Capture and PII Risk:** This project captures ambient audio from a public street in San Francisco's Mission District to identify songs using Shazam. The primary output is song metadata (title, artist, timestamp), but the process inherently involves recording short audio clips. This creates a significant risk of incidentally capturing intelligible human speech, identifiable vocal characteristics, or other sonic identifiers. The project's own page acknowledges the lack of consent, stating, "This is culture surveillance. No one notices, no one consents." While the public-facing site does not publish raw audio, the potential retention of these audio clips by the device, an intermediary service mentioned as "Orchestra," or Shazam itself poses a substantial privacy risk.
*   **Legal-Ethical Context:** In the U.S., particularly California, the legality hinges on the expectation of privacy. California's wiretapping law (Penal Code §632) requires all-party consent for recording "confidential communications." However, conversations in a public space where there is no reasonable expectation of privacy generally fall outside this statute. While likely legal under this interpretation, the project raises significant ethical questions about continuous, surreptitious audio surveillance in a residential neighborhood, the potential for chilling effects on public expression, and the normalization of mass audio collection, even under the guise of art.
*   **Consent Feasibility:** Obtaining meaningful consent from every passerby is practically impossible, a fact the project's creator explicitly notes. The project's intent is framed as artistic and cultural commentary, but this does not negate the privacy implications for those unknowingly recorded.

**2. Find My Parking Cops (SFMTA Citation Scraping):**
*   **Data Capture and PII Risk:** This project scraped the SFMTA's public citation payment portal to reconstruct the near-real-time locations of parking enforcement officers. The scraped data included officer identifiers, the precise street address of the citation, and timestamps. Critically, the underlying citation pages also contained PII of ticketed individuals, such as vehicle make and license plate numbers. The SFMTA's official response confirmed that the project put at risk the "personal information of people who have received parking citations."
*   **Legal-Ethical Context:** The project operated in a legal gray area regarding web scraping. While the *hiQ v. LinkedIn* case suggests scraping publicly accessible data may not violate the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA), this is not a blanket protection. The SFMTA's rapid action to shut down the data feed demonstrates that data owners can and will act to protect what they perceive as sensitive operational data and PII. Ethically, the project crossed a significant boundary by transforming public administrative records into a near-real-time tracking tool for government employees, creating foreseeable risks of harassment, stalking, or evasion of law enforcement.
*   **Consent Feasibility:** Consent was not obtained from either the parking officers being tracked or the citizens whose citation data was scraped. Given the nature of the project, obtaining such consent would be infeasible.

**3. J* Interfaces (Epstein-Related Public Documents):**
*   **Data Capture and PII Risk:** This suite of projects repackages publicly released government documents related to the "Epstein files" into user-friendly interfaces (e.g., Jmail, JPhotos). These documents are public records but contain extremely sensitive PII, including names, contact details, and associations of victims, witnesses, and other individuals. While some official releases may have redactions, the act of making this data easily searchable and cross-referenceable significantly amplifies the risk of re-identification and reputational harm, particularly for victims. Reports indicate that victims have previously sued government agencies for the release of their identifying information in similar document dumps.
*   **Legal-Ethical Context:** In the U.S., republishing lawfully obtained public records is generally protected. However, the ethical considerations are profound. The project re-amplifies trauma and privacy invasions for victims and other non-public figures mentioned in the files. While the stated intent is to provide transparency and archival access, the outcome is the mass-accessibility of sensitive personal histories without the consent of those involved. Depending on the individuals in the dataset and the project's operations, data protection regimes like GDPR or CCPA could theoretically apply.
*   **Consent Feasibility:** Consent is not a factor, as the data is sourced from public government releases. The ethical burden falls on the re-publisher to consider and mitigate the harms of amplification.
content.analysis_of_real_time_tracking_and_sourc:
An evaluation of Riley Walz's projects, particularly 'Find My Parking Cops' and 'Bop Spotter', reveals a significant focus on near-real-time data publication, which in turn exposes the inherent fragility of the public data sources they rely upon.

**1. Real-Time Tracking Mechanisms:**
*   **Find My Parking Cops:** This project achieved near-real-time tracking of San Francisco parking officers through a clever and efficient scraping mechanism. Walz reverse-engineered the pattern of citation ID generation, discovering a predictable sequence (e.g., "add 11, except add 4 if the last digit is 6"). He also inferred that officers' devices claimed ticket IDs in batches of about 100. By combining these insights, his scraper could efficiently poll for the next likely ticket ID for each officer, making a request only "every few seconds." Since the SFMTA's portal published ticket details almost immediately after they were written, this system could reconstruct officer locations with a latency of minutes or even seconds. The public-facing map displayed these locations, creating a live feed of enforcement activity.
*   **Bop Spotter:** This project operates a continuous, near-real-time feed of cultural data. An installation with a microphone runs Shazam "24 hours a day, 7 days a week," logging identified songs with minute-level timestamps. The public data shows multiple detections within single minutes, indicating a very low latency between a song being played on the street and it appearing in the project's public log. While not tracking individuals, it provides a live pulse of a neighborhood's auditory environment.

**2. Plausible Misuse Scenarios:**
The near-real-time nature of these projects enables several plausible misuse scenarios, with 'Find My Parking Cops' presenting the most acute risks:
*   **Evasion of Enforcement:** The most obvious use case, and the one Walz himself alluded to, was for drivers to use the live map to avoid parking in areas with recent officer activity, thereby undermining the city's enforcement objectives and revenue.
*   **Harassment and Stalking:** The ability to track the specific movements of individual officers creates a significant safety risk. Malicious actors could use the data to harass, intimidate, or even plan physical attacks on officers. The publication of officer identifiers and "leaderboards" could facilitate targeted public shaming.
*   **Privacy Invasion of Third Parties:** The scraped citation data included license plates and vehicle information, exposing the locations of private citizens at specific times to anyone viewing the map.

**3. Source Fragility and the SFMTA's Rapid Response:**
'Find My Parking Cops' serves as a powerful case study in source fragility. The project's reliance on scraping a public-facing, but not officially supported, data presentation layer made it extremely vulnerable.
*   **Timeline of Response:** The project launched on September 23, 2025. Within approximately four hours, the SFMTA altered its website, causing the scraper to receive an "unauthorized" error and effectively disabling the real-time data feed. The project page now exists as a static snapshot, with a note from Walz stating, "This site only worked for 4 hours until the SFMTA cut the data source I was using."
*   **SFMTA's Justification:** The agency's response was swift and decisive. In a public statement, the SFMTA cited risks to "staff’s safety, and personal information of people who have received parking citations" as the reason for their action. They asserted their commitment to transparency through official channels like DataSF but drew a hard line against unauthorized uses that create safety risks.
*   **Analysis:** This incident demonstrates the significant leverage data owners have over projects that rely on scraping. By making minor changes to their website's structure, authentication, or data presentation, an agency can instantly break a scraper. This highlights that while scraping public data may be technically possible and exist in a legal gray area, it is an inherently brittle foundation for any service. The creator's accountability is not absolved by the data owner's action; the incident raises questions about whether the creator should have anticipated these risks and implemented mitigations (like a time delay) from the outset.
content.analysis_of_social_scoring_potential:
While Riley Walz's projects do not appear to explicitly implement any form of social scoring system, the nature of the data they collect, aggregate, and publish creates a significant potential for third parties to use these datasets for social profiling and the creation of reputational scores. The projects effectively serve as pre-processed data sources that could fuel such systems.

*   **Find My Parking Cops:** This project carries a high potential for enabling profiling, primarily of the parking enforcement officers themselves. By logging the precise time and location of every ticket issued by an identifiable officer, a third party could easily:
    *   **Create Performance and Behavior Profiles:** Analyze an officer's ticketing frequency, geographic patterns, and adherence to shift schedules. This could be used to create unofficial "leaderboards" or reputational scores based on enforcement activity, potentially for harassment or public shaming.
    *   **Infer Personal Patterns:** By analyzing the start and end points of ticketing routes over time, one could attempt to infer an officer's work base, or even their home neighborhood, creating serious personal security risks.
    *   **Profile Ticketed Individuals:** While the primary focus was on officers, the incidental collection of license plate and location data could be used to build profiles of citizens' movements and habits.

*   **J* Interfaces:** The J* suite presents a very high risk for enabling social profiling and reputational scoring. The source material—publicly released documents from the Epstein case—is rich with information about personal relationships, communications, and financial transactions. By placing this corpus into an easily searchable and cross-referenceable interface, the project dramatically lowers the barrier for third parties to:
    *   **Construct Social Graphs:** Map out the relationships and frequency of communication between Jeffrey Epstein and a wide network of public and private individuals.
    *   **Generate Reputational Scores:** Third parties could easily assign scores to individuals based on their proximity to or frequency of interaction within the dataset, regardless of the context or nature of the interaction. This could be used for political attacks, public shaming, or blacklisting.
    *   **Enable Doxxing and Harassment:** The aggregation of names, dates, and locations from disparate documents makes it easier for malicious actors to compile dossiers on individuals for targeted harassment.

*   **Bop Spotter:** The potential for social scoring from Bop Spotter is more indirect but still present. On its own, a list of songs detected on a street corner has low profiling potential. However, if this data were combined with other sources, it could contribute to a larger profile:
    *   **Neighborhood Profiling:** The dataset provides a real-time feed of a neighborhood's "vibe," which could be used by real estate companies, marketers, or researchers to create cultural or demographic profiles of the area.
    *   **Individual Profiling (with correlation):** If a third party could correlate the song data with other data streams (e.g., traffic camera footage, social media check-ins, cell phone location data), they could potentially link musical tastes to specific individuals or groups, contributing to a more detailed consumer or social profile.

In summary, Walz's work demonstrates that even without the intent to create a social scoring system, the act of collecting and publishing granular, identity-linked, or easily correlated data can provide the raw material for others to do so. The primary risk lies in the downstream, unanticipated uses of these novel datasets.
content.analysis_of_transparency_and_disclaimers:
Riley Walz's projects exhibit a mixed and evolving approach to transparency and disclaimers, ranging from minimal legalistic statements to surprisingly detailed technical disclosures, which in turn create a complex trade-off between aiding public understanding and increasing replication risk.

**1. Sufficiency and Nature of Disclaimers:**
The disclaimers on Walz's projects often serve more as statements of non-affiliation and artistic framing rather than as comprehensive warnings or risk mitigations.
*   **Find My Parking Cops:** The project page featured a concise disclaimer: "By Riley Walz. Not affiliated with the San Francisco government or Apple's Find My." This statement effectively clarifies brand and legal separation but does nothing to address the primary risks identified by the SFMTA, such as officer safety, harassment, or the privacy of ticketed individuals. It is a legally prudent but ethically insufficient disclaimer.
*   **Bop Spotter:** This project eschews a traditional disclaimer in favor of a provocative artistic statement: "This is culture surveillance. No one notices, no one consents." This is a form of transparency about intent and the ethical posture of the work, but it does not function as a risk mitigation. Instead, it leans into the controversy as part of the project's commentary.
*   **J* Interfaces:** In contrast, the J* suite demonstrates a more mature approach. Its 'about' page reportedly includes an explicit redaction policy detailing what content is withheld (e.g., victim identities, explicit imagery) and provides contact channels for remediation requests. This form of transparency is far more effective because it is paired with a concrete harm reduction mechanism.

**2. Effects of Transparency: Public Understanding vs. Replication Risk:**
Walz is often transparent about his methods, both on project pages and in media interviews. However, the level of detail provided has varying effects.
*   **Aiding Understanding:** High-level descriptions of his projects—such as explaining that 'Find My Parking Cops' works by scraping public citation pages—are valuable for public discourse. They expose the vulnerabilities of public data systems and allow for a debate on transparency versus security. This level of disclosure serves a legitimate public interest and accountability function.
*   **Increasing Replication Risk:** The most significant issue arises with 'Find My Parking Cops', where Walz went beyond a high-level description. On the project's 'about' page and in interviews, he disclosed specific, actionable operational details of his scraper. This included the predictable algorithm for generating ticket numbers ("add 11, except add 4 if the last digit is 6"), the batching behavior of officer devices, and the efficient polling strategy ("check the first ticket in each batch"). This level of detail is not necessary for public understanding and effectively serves as a blueprint for anyone else looking to replicate the near-real-time tracking system. It materially lowers the technical barrier to recreating the very tool that posed a safety risk, transforming transparency into a potential vector for proliferation.

**Conclusion:**
The non-affiliation disclaimers used in projects like 'Find My Parking Cops' are largely insufficient as they only address legal positioning and fail to engage with the substantive ethical and safety risks. While transparency is a core part of Walz's work, his disclosures demonstrate a critical boundary: transparency about *intent and high-level methods* aids public understanding, but transparency about *specific, operational heuristics and algorithms* can dangerously increase replication risk and enable abuse. The J* suite's model of pairing transparency with an explicit, actionable redaction policy and remediation channel represents a much more responsible and effective practice.
content.analysis_of_remediation_and_takedown_pat:
The ecosystem of Riley Walz's projects demonstrates a significant gap in formal, creator-provided remediation and takedown pathways, with the most effective interventions coming from external agencies rather than internal mechanisms.

**1. Availability of Opt-Out and Takedown Mechanisms:**
*   **Find My Parking Cops & Bop Spotter:** For these key projects, there is no evidence of any formal or structured opt-out, redaction, or takedown mechanism being offered on the project pages themselves. There were no visible privacy policies, contact forms for abuse reporting, or automated tools for individuals to request the removal of their data (e.g., a ticketed citizen or a person whose conversation was captured). The primary avenue for contact appears to be the creator's public social media handle, which is an informal and unstructured channel not suitable for managing sensitive remediation requests.
*   **J* Interfaces:** In contrast, the J* suite reportedly includes a more structured approach. Its 'about' page is said to contain a redaction policy and provides contact channels (X, Instagram, TikTok) for users to request that content be redacted or un-redacted. This represents a clear, albeit platform-dependent, pathway for remediation that is absent in the other projects.

**2. Effectiveness of Remediation Pathways and Agency Interventions:**
The most prominent and effective remediation event was not initiated by the creator but by an external data owner.
*   **SFMTA Intervention:** The 'Find My Parking Cops' project was effectively shut down within approximately four hours of its launch. The San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency (SFMTA) acted swiftly to alter its public-facing website, which broke the scraper's ability to access the near-real-time data. The SFMTA's public statement confirmed this was a deliberate action taken due to concerns for "staff’s safety, and personal information of people who have received parking citations." This incident highlights that for projects scraping public data, the most powerful remediation lever is often held by the data owner, who can modify the source at will.
*   **Creator Responsiveness:** The creator's response was reactive. After the SFMTA's intervention, the 'Find My Parking Cops' page was updated to show a static snapshot with a note explaining that the data source had been cut. There is no evidence of the creator proactively offering takedowns or implementing mitigations (like a time delay) in response to the foreseeable safety concerns prior to the agency's action.
*   **Platform Moderation:** There is no public record in the provided sources of moderation actions taken by platforms (like web hosts or domain registrars) against Walz's projects.

**Conclusion:**
The remediation pathways within Walz's ecosystem are largely underdeveloped and informal. For high-risk projects like 'Find My Parking Cops', the burden of remediation fell entirely on the external data owner (SFMTA), which responded with a technical shutdown rather than a policy dialogue or a formal takedown request. This reactive posture, where remediation is dependent on an external party's intervention, is a significant flaw. The lack of clear, accessible, and reliable channels for individuals or agencies to report harm and request removal places the subjects of the data at a disadvantage and fails to meet a basic standard of ethical data stewardship for experimental projects.

synthesis_of_public_criticism_and_suppor.stakeholder_group: City Agencies (e.g., San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency - SFMTA)
synthesis_of_public_criticism_and_suppor.perceived_benefits: This stakeholder group perceived no benefits from the project. Their public statements and actions focused exclusively on the risks and the need to control data access. While transparency advocates might argue for the benefit of public oversight, the agency itself framed the project as an unauthorized and dangerous exploitation of their data systems.
synthesis_of_public_criticism_and_suppor.perceived_harms_or_criticisms: The primary harms identified were severe risks to operational and personal safety. The SFMTA was concerned that the near-real-time tracking of parking enforcement officers could be used to harass, stalk, or otherwise endanger staff. They also cited risks to the privacy of the public, as the scraped citation pages contained 'personal information of people who have received parking citations,' including vehicle and license plate details. The project was seen as enabling the evasion of enforcement, undermining the agency's regulatory function, and representing an unauthorized use of their data infrastructure.
synthesis_of_public_criticism_and_suppor.representative_quote_or_action: The most representative action was the SFMTA's decision to alter its website and cut off the data source for 'Find My Parking Cops' within approximately four hours of the project's launch on September 23, 2025. This was accompanied by a public statement which read, in part: '...when our staff’s safety, and personal information of people who have received parking citations, is at risk, we must act on that swiftly.' This rapid and decisive technical intervention underscores the high level of perceived harm.

content.proposed_safety_model_overview: This detailed, Riley-inspired safety model is specifically tailored for experimental, public-facing data and art projects that handle ambient media and scraped public records. It is structured around explicit principles, technical controls, governance processes, and operational runbooks. The model is derived from an analysis of Riley Walz's projects, such as Bop Spotter and Find My Parking Cops, which highlighted significant risks associated with real-time tracking, incidental PII capture, and the fragility of public data sources. The model's goal is to provide concrete, actionable guidance that goes beyond generic guardrails by specifying default thresholds and processes. It prioritizes harm elimination, data minimization, and transparency to help creators preserve the experimental and archival value of their projects while substantially reducing direct harms to individuals, officials, and communities.

safety_model_core_principles.description: The foremost principle is to avoid publishing information that could enable stalking, harassment, doxxing, or real-time targeting of individuals or officers. If a control cannot eliminate a high-risk harm, the information should not be published.
Only collect the necessary data fields to achieve the artistic or analytic objective. Raw personal identifiers should be discarded as early as possible, with a preference for ephemeral capture.
Clearly state the intended uses of the data in a public data charter and restrict any downstream uses that are inconsistent with that charter.
Publish the project's design, data sources, limitations, and provide clear opt-out channels. Seek community advisory input for projects situated in public spaces.

Ambient media (audio/video of public spaces) and scraped public records (records with structured Personally Identifiable Information - PII) should be treated differently. Ambient media often requires stronger temporal/spatial blurring and immediate anonymization.
In cases of doubt, default to more protective settings, such as longer delays or coarser aggregation.

safety_model_technical_controls.description: This control mandates a default publication delay to mitigate risks from real-time tracking. A default publication delay of at least 72 hours is required for ambient audio-derived events. For scraped discrete records that expose real-time location or activity of individuals (e.g., enforcement citations), a minimum 24–72 hour embargo is required. This control directly addresses the primary risk observed in projects like 'Find My Parking Cops,' where near-real-time data enabled potential harassment and evasion of enforcement.
safety_model_technical_controls.default_parameter: A default publication delay of >=72 hours for ambient audio events, and a minimum of >=24–72 hour embargo for scraped records exposing real-time activity.

safety_model_governance_processes.process_name: Mandatory Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) + Legal Review
safety_model_governance_processes.description: This is a formal, structured process designed to identify, assess, and mitigate privacy risks associated with a data project before its launch. The PIA involves creating a comprehensive data flow diagram, categorizing the data being processed, analyzing potential privacy threats and their likelihood, defining and documenting mitigation measures, and assessing the residual risk. It is paired with a mandatory legal review to ensure the project's data collection and publication methods comply with relevant laws and regulations, such as the Computer Fraud and Abuse Act (CFAA) for scraping activities, state-specific wiretapping laws for audio collection, and terms of service of data sources. The objective is to shift from a reactive posture (waiting for a takedown) to a proactive, risk-aware development cycle.
safety_model_governance_processes.requirements: This process is mandatory and must be triggered for any project that meets one or more of the following criteria: 1) The project involves the collection of ambient media, such as audio or video, in public or semi-public spaces. 2) The project scrapes or otherwise automatically collects data from websites, portals, or APIs not explicitly designed or licensed for bulk automated access. 3) The project's output is capable of reconstructing or visualizing the movements of individuals, including government staff, in near-real-time. 4) The project plans to store raw, unredacted Personally Identifiable Information (PII) for longer than a default ephemeral period of 72 hours.

safety_model_operational_runbooks.runbook_component: Remediation SLA Tier for Critical Threats
safety_model_operational_runbooks.details: For incoming requests triaged as critical, the service level agreement (SLA) mandates a response and mitigation within 6 hours for a temporary takedown of the harmful content or feature. This is followed by a 24-hour window to implement a root-cause fix (e.g., deploying a permanent redaction filter, adding a publication delay). A public-facing incident report must be published within 72 hours, detailing the nature of the issue (without revealing sensitive information), the actions taken, and preventative measures being implemented.

safety_model_application_to_walz_project.risks_before_model_application: The project presented multiple high-severity risks in its original form. The primary risk was enabling the near-real-time tracking of government employees (parking officers) by scraping citation data that was published immediately. This created a direct physical safety risk, enabling potential harassment, stalking, or targeted evasion of enforcement. A secondary risk was the incidental exposure of Personally Identifiable Information (PII) of ticketed citizens, as citation pages could contain vehicle license plates and makes, linked to precise locations and times. The project also demonstrated extreme source fragility, as it relied on an unofficial, unstable data access method that the SFMTA quickly disabled. Finally, it lacked any remediation path, such as an opt-out or takedown process, placing the entire burden of mitigation on the data owner (SFMTA).
safety_model_application_to_walz_project.mitigations_from_model: Applying the proposed safety model would have mandated several critical controls before launch. First, the 'Mandatory PIA + Legal Review' governance process would have been triggered, identifying the high risk of real-time tracking. Second, the pre-launch gating question, 'Does the output enable real-time evasion, harassment, or targeted action?' would have been answered 'Yes,' forcing the application of technical controls. These would include: 1) A **Temporal Delay**, enforcing a 24-72 hour embargo on publishing citation data, transforming the tool from a real-time tracker to a historical analysis platform. 2) **Spatial Aggregation**, displaying data as heatmaps aggregated to the city block or neighborhood level instead of precise street addresses. 3) **PII Redaction**, automatically stripping or hashing license plates and officer identifiers from public view. 4) A **Public Data Charter** would be published, transparently stating the data is delayed and aggregated.
safety_model_application_to_walz_project.risk_profile_after_model_application: After applying the safety model's controls, the project's risk profile would be substantially lowered from 'High' to 'Low-to-Medium'. The primary risk of real-time tracking and physical harm to officers would be eliminated. The project's utility would shift from operational evasion to legitimate public oversight and accountability research, analyzing historical enforcement patterns. The privacy risk to ticketed individuals would be mitigated through PII redaction. While the project would still be subject to source fragility, the proactive governance process might have encouraged engagement with the SFMTA for a more stable, official data source. The project would be a defensible transparency tool rather than a controversial real-time tracking application.

safety_model_compliance_and_auditing.description: This metric measures the effectiveness of the automated PII stripping and redaction filters. It is calculated by taking a labeled sample of the source data containing known PII (e.g., license plates, names, phone numbers) and measuring the percentage of those identifiers that were correctly detected and redacted by the system before publication. A high recall rate is critical to minimizing the risk of inadvertent PII disclosure.
safety_model_compliance_and_auditing.target: The target for this metric is a recall rate of greater than 95% for direct, structured identifiers. This means the system must successfully identify and redact at least 95 out of every 100 instances of PII in the test set.
safety_model_compliance_and_auditing.review_frequency: This metric should be reviewed and audited on a monthly basis. The audit involves running the redaction system against a new, held-out labeled dataset to prevent overfitting and ensure its performance remains high as data sources and formats evolve.

content.key_ethical_tensions_identified: Riley Walz's work consistently highlights several recurring ethical tensions at the intersection of technology, art, and public data. The most prominent is the conflict between **transparency versus safety**, starkly illustrated by the 'Find My Parking Cops' project. This tension pits the public interest value of government oversight against the real-world safety risks to individuals (like government employees) whose movements are tracked and published in near-real-time. Another key tension is the **ethics of repurposing public records**. Walz's projects demonstrate that even when data is legally public (e.g., citation records, court documents), aggregating and re-contextualizing it into easily accessible, high-risk formats (like a real-time map or a searchable email archive) creates new ethical dilemmas and potential harms not present in the original, disparate sources. This challenges the simple notion that 'public data is fair game.' Finally, there is a recurring debate around the **use of art as a justification for surveillance-like projects**. Projects like 'Bop Spotter' are framed as 'culture surveillance' or artistic commentary, which raises the question of whether artistic intent is a sufficient ethical defense for activities involving nonconsensual data collection, such as recording ambient audio in public spaces, and the potential privacy invasions that result.
content.unverified_project_details: During the research process, the project names 'Billionaire Detector' and 'Find My Vibe' were investigated. However, comprehensive searches across authoritative sources, including Riley Walz's personal portfolio website (walzr.com), archived web pages, and extensive press coverage from outlets like Wired, NPR, and Mission Local, did not yield any public project pages or definitive mentions of projects with these exact titles. Therefore, these names could not be authoritatively verified as official project titles. The research concludes that these are most likely informal descriptive labels, misremembered names, or aliases for other projects within Walz's body of work.

executive_summary.description: A high-level overview of Riley Walz's approach to safety and ethics in his public-facing projects, summarizing the key patterns of risk and the core components of the proposed safety model.

overall_assessment_of_walz_approach.description: A direct assessment of how Riley Walz's projects handle or fail to handle the specific areas of concern: privacy, consent, public data, real-time tracking, social scoring, non-affiliation, caveats, opt-outs, source fragility, takedowns, and public criticism.

project_name.description: The official name of the project.

description.description: A concise description of the project's purpose and function.

current_status.description: The operational status of the project as of the analysis date (e.g., Active, Defunct, Archived).

data_sources_and_methods.description: The data sources used (e.g., public APIs, scraped web pages) and the methods of collection.

data_sensitivity_and_pii_exposure.description: Analysis of the data's sensitivity and the extent of Personally Identifiable Information (PII) exposure.

real_time_characteristics.description: The degree to which the project publishes data in real-time or near-real-time.

disclaimers_and_transparency.description: Verbatim disclaimers or transparency statements provided by the project.

outcomes_and_reactions.description: Notable outcomes, such as media coverage, public reaction, or interventions by agencies or platforms.

project_inventory.description: A comprehensive inventory of Riley Walz's public-facing projects. Each project entry should include its purpose, data sources, collection methods, data sensitivity, PII exposure, real-time characteristics, disclaimers, opt-out availability, and notable outcomes such as media coverage or agency responses.

analysis_of_privacy_and_consent.description: A detailed analysis of how anchor projects (Bop Spotter, Find My Parking Cops, J* interfaces) handle privacy and consent, considering U.S. legal-ethical contexts, incidental PII capture, and the feasibility of obtaining consent.

analysis_of_real_time_tracking_and_sourc.description: An evaluation of real-time tracking mechanisms and source fragility, focusing on Find My Parking Cops and Bop Spotter. This includes the technical methods for near-real-time publication, plausible misuse scenarios, and an analysis of the SFMTA's rapid response.

analysis_of_social_scoring_potential.description: Assessment of the potential for Walz's projects to enable third-party social scoring or profiling, even if not explicitly implemented. This covers how datasets could be used to create reputational scores or profiles.

analysis_of_transparency_and_disclaimers.description: An assessment of the sufficiency and effects of non-affiliation disclaimers, caveats, and transparency practices across Walz's projects and interviews, evaluating whether they aid understanding or increase replication risk.

analysis_of_remediation_and_takedown_pat.description: An investigation into the availability and effectiveness of opt-out, takedown, and remediation pathways in Walz's ecosystem, including platform moderation, agency interventions, and creator responsiveness.

stakeholder_group.description: The group providing the feedback (e.g., City Agencies, Privacy Ethicists, General Public, Journalists).

perceived_benefits.description: The positive aspects or benefits of the project as perceived by this stakeholder group.

perceived_harms_or_criticisms.description: The negative aspects, harms, or criticisms of the project as perceived by this stakeholder group.

representative_quote_or_action.description: A quote or summary of an action that represents the group's sentiment.

proposed_safety_model_overview.description: An introduction to the detailed, Riley-inspired safety model tailored for experimental, public-facing data and art projects.

principle_name.description: The name of the core principle.

description.description: A detailed explanation of the principle and its importance.

safety_model_core_principles.description: A list of the explicit, prioritized principles of the safety model, such as harm elimination, data minimization, and purpose limitation.

control_name.description: The name of the technical control (e.g., Temporal Delay, Spatial Aggregation).

description.description: A description of what the control does and why it is important.

default_parameter.description: The specific, recommended default setting or threshold for the control (e.g., '>=24-72 hour delay', 'Block-level aggregation').

process_name.description: The name of the governance process (e.g., Privacy Impact Assessment, External Advisory Review).

description.description: An explanation of the process and its objectives.

requirements.description: Specific requirements or triggers for when this process must be enacted.

runbook_component.description: The specific component of the operational runbook (e.g., Pre-Launch Gating Question, Remediation SLA Tier).

details.description: The specific details of the runbook component, such as the question to be asked or the response time for an SLA tier.

tier_or_category.description: The category or priority level, if applicable (e.g., 'Critical Threat', 'High Risk PII').

project_name.description: The name of the project being analyzed.

risks_before_model_application.description: A summary of the key safety and ethical risks the project presented in its original form.

mitigations_from_model.description: The specific controls from the safety model that would be applied to mitigate the identified risks.

risk_profile_after_model_application.description: An assessment of the project's residual risk after the safety model's controls are applied.

metric_name.description: The name of the key performance indicator or compliance metric.

description.description: A description of what the metric measures.

target.description: The target value or state for this metric (e.g., '>95% recall', '<6 hours response time').

review_frequency.description: How often this metric should be reviewed or audited (e.g., Monthly, Quarterly).

key_ethical_tensions_identified.description: A summary of the recurring ethical tensions highlighted by Walz's work, such as transparency versus safety, the ethics of repurposing public records, and the use of art as a justification for surveillance-like projects.

unverified_project_details.description: A note on project names mentioned in initial research that could not be authoritatively verified, such as 'Billionaire Detector' and 'Find My Vibe', clarifying them as likely aliases or informal references.
